description: Original tasks before rewriting based on actual data

tasks.name: Create a reading list with 3 reinforcement learning journal articles (2019–2023) with ≥50 citations
tasks.description: On the researcher's publication page, find journal articles about reinforcement learning published between 2019 and 2023 that have at least 50 citations, and add exactly 3 of them to a new reading list named 'Thesis Sources'. Success is measured by having a reading list called 'Thesis Sources' that contains exactly 3 items matching the year, topic, type, and citation-count criteria.
steps: Navigate to the website homepage and click on the main navigation link labeled 'Publications'.
In the publications page search bar, type 'reinforcement learning' and press Enter or click the search icon to apply the keyword filter.
Locate the publication type filters (e.g., checkboxes or dropdown) and select only the 'Journal article' option so that other types such as 'Conference paper' or 'Book chapter' are deselected.
Find the year range filter controls and set the minimum year to '2019' and the maximum year to '2023'.
Locate the citation count filter (e.g., a slider or numeric input) and set the minimum citation count to '50', leaving the maximum value unchanged or at its default.
Open the sort menu for the results list and select the option 'Citations – High to Low' so the most cited items appear at the top.
In the filtered and sorted results list, use the selection control (e.g., checkbox) to select the first 3 publications shown that match the current filters.

Click the 'Add to list' or 'Save to reading list' button above or below the results, choose 'Create new list', enter the name 'Thesis Sources' in the list name field, and confirm to save the 3 selected publications into this new list.

tasks.name: Save the APA citation of the most recent graph neural network conference paper into a notes section
tasks.description: Locate the most recently published conference paper on graph neural networks by this researcher, generate its citation in APA format, and copy that citation text into an on-site notes area named 'Clipboard' or similar. Success is measured by having the APA citation text for the single most recent matching conference paper stored as a new note.
steps: From any page, click the 'Publications' link in the site header to open the full publications list.
In the search or keyword field on the publications page, type 'graph neural network' and execute the search by pressing Enter or clicking the search icon.
Use the publication type filter controls and select only 'Conference paper' so that journals and other types are not included.
Open the sort dropdown for the filtered list and select 'Year – Newest First' so that the most recent conference paper appears at the top.
Click on the title of the first publication in the list (the most recent conference paper) to open its detailed publication page.
On the publication detail page, click the 'Cite' or 'Citation' button, then in the citation format options choose 'APA' as the citation style.
In the APA citation box that appears, use the 'Copy' button or manually select the entire citation text and copy it to the clipboard using the browser's copy command.

Open the site's notes or 'Clipboard' feature (e.g., by clicking a 'Notes' icon in the header), click 'New note' or an equivalent button, paste the copied APA citation into the note content field, and save the note with the title 'GNN latest conference paper'.

tasks.name: Bookmark the higher-funded climate modeling project that has at least 4 team members
tasks.description: On the Projects section of the profile, compare the first two climate modeling projects returned by a keyword search and bookmark the one that has the larger funding amount and a team size of at least 4 members. Success is measured by having exactly one bookmarked project that meets both the higher-funding and minimum-team-size criteria.
steps: Navigate to the homepage and click the 'Research Projects' or 'Projects' link in the main navigation menu.
In the projects search bar, type 'climate modeling' and run the search to filter the project list.
In the filtered results, identify the first two projects in the list and open each of them in separate tabs or windows by Ctrl-clicking (or equivalent) on their titles.
Switch to the first project’s detail tab, locate the 'Funding amount' field and the 'Team members' section, and note the funding value and count how many team members are listed.
Switch to the second project’s detail tab, again locate the 'Funding amount' field and the 'Team members' section, and note the funding value and the team member count for this project.
Decide which of the two projects has the higher funding amount and also has at least 4 team members listed (team size ≥ 4); choose that project as the one to bookmark.
Return to the chosen project’s detail tab and click the 'Bookmark' or 'Save project' control (such as a star icon or 'Add to bookmarks' button).
If a folder selection dialog appears, select or create a folder named 'Potential collaborations' and confirm so that the selected higher-funded, ≥4-member project is saved to that folder.

tasks.name: Create a 'Courses to Take 2025' list with 2 graduate Spring 2025 courses worth at least 3 credits
tasks.description: Use the Teaching section to find graduate-level courses offered in Spring 2025 that are worth at least 3 credits and add exactly 2 of them to a new list named 'Courses to Take 2025'. If more than 2 courses match, choose the two with the lowest course numbers. Success is measured by the 'Courses to Take 2025' list containing exactly 2 matching courses.
steps: From the site's main navigation, click on the 'Teaching' or 'Courses' link to open the list of courses taught by the researcher.
Locate the term or semester filter and set it to 'Spring 2025' so that only courses offered in that term are displayed.
Find the course level filter (e.g., Undergraduate/Graduate) and select 'Graduate' while ensuring that 'Undergraduate' or other levels are not selected.
Use the credits or units filter to set the minimum credits to '3' (keeping the maximum at its default) so that only courses with 3 or more credits remain in the list.
Open the sort dropdown for the filtered course list and choose to sort by 'Course number – Low to High' or equivalent so that lower-numbered courses appear first.
From the resulting list, select the first two courses that appear by clicking on a selection control (e.g., checkbox) next to each course title.
Click the 'Add to list' or 'Save selection' button above or below the course list, choose 'Create new list', and type 'Courses to Take 2025' in the list name field.
Confirm the action so that these exactly 2 selected graduate Spring 2025 courses with at least 3 credits are saved into the newly created 'Courses to Take 2025' list.

tasks.name: Add the earliest upcoming public talk in the next 6 months (New York or Online) to an Attend list with a 3-day reminder
tasks.description: Within the Talks or Events section, find public talks occurring in the next 6 months that are either located in New York or listed as Online, select the one with the earliest date, and add it to an 'Attend' list with a reminder set 3 days before the event. Success is measured by having one event in the 'Attend' list with a reminder set for 3 days prior.
steps: Click on the 'Talks', 'Events', or 'Speaking' link in the site's main navigation to open the list of upcoming and past events.
Use the date range filter to set the start date to today's date and the end date to exactly 6 months from today, so only events within the next 6 months are shown.
Find the location filter and select options that include 'New York' and 'Online', making sure other locations are deselected so only these two locations remain in the results.
If there is a 'Type' or 'Access' filter, choose 'Public' to exclude private or invitation-only events from the list.
Open the sort dropdown and set it to 'Date – Soonest First' so that the event happening the earliest within the filtered range appears at the top.
From the filtered and sorted list, click on the title of the first event shown (the earliest upcoming public talk in New York or Online) to open its event detail page.
On the event detail page, click the 'Add to Attend list', 'Save event', or similar button, and if prompted, select or create a list named 'Attend' to store this event.
After saving the event, click the 'Set reminder' or 'Notifications' option for this event and choose a reminder time of '3 days before', then confirm the reminder setting.

tasks.name: Submit a supervision request via the contact form with the earliest Wednesday 10:00–12:00 slot
tasks.description: Use the website's contact/supervision request form to send a message as a prospective PhD student, selecting the earliest available Wednesday time slot between 10:00 and 12:00 shown in the on-site scheduler. Success is measured by a submitted form containing the specified role, message content, and a chosen slot that meets the weekday and time-window criteria.
steps: From the homepage, click on the 'Contact', 'Supervision', or 'Get in touch' link in the main navigation to open the contact form page.
In the contact reason dropdown or radio buttons, select the option labeled 'Prospective PhD student' or the closest equivalent.
Fill in the name field with 'Alex Rivera' and the email field with 'alex.rivera@example.edu' (entered directly into the form inputs without using any external email service).

In the subject field, type 'PhD Supervision Inquiry for Fall 2026' and in the main message text area, enter a short message of at least one sentence describing your research interest in machine learning.
Scroll to the scheduling or 'Preferred meeting time' section and open the embedded availability picker or calendar widget.
Within the scheduler, view the next several weeks and locate the earliest available Wednesday that shows at least one time slot starting at or after 10:00 and ending by 12:00.
Select the earliest time slot on that Wednesday that falls entirely between 10:00 and 12:00 (for example, 10:00–10:30 if that is the first option in this window).
Once all fields are filled and the appropriate time slot is selected, click the 'Submit', 'Send message', or equivalent button to send the supervision request through the website.

tasks.name: Update the profile tagline and set exactly three primary research keywords in account settings
tasks.description: As the site owner, sign in using the on-site login form, update the public profile tagline to a specific sentence, and set exactly three primary research keywords. Success is measured by an updated tagline and a saved list of exactly three keywords in the profile settings.
steps: Click the 'Sign in' or 'Login' link in the site header to open the on-site login form.
In the username field, type 'researcher_admin', and in the password field, type 'SecurePass123' (both entered directly into the website form), then click the 'Sign in' button.
After signing in, open the user menu (for example, by clicking your avatar or name in the header) and select 'Profile settings' or 'Account settings' from the dropdown.

On the profile settings page, locate the 'Tagline' or 'Short bio line' input field, delete any existing text, and replace it with the new sentence: 'I study how machine learning can make scientific discovery faster and more reliable.'.
Scroll to the section labeled 'Primary research keywords', 'Research tags', or similar, and remove any existing keywords so that the list is empty.
In the first keyword input, type 'reinforcement learning'; in the second, type 'causal inference'; and in the third, type 'scientific discovery', ensuring that only these three keywords are present.
If the interface requires confirmation for new keywords (e.g., pressing Enter or clicking an 'Add' button), confirm each of the three keywords so they appear as separate tags or chips.
Click the 'Save changes' or 'Update profile' button at the bottom or top of the settings page to store the new tagline and the three primary research keywords.

tasks.name: Limit the public publications list to the 5 most-cited items and feature 1 post-2021 paper
tasks.description: Configure the public profile display settings so that only the 5 most-cited publications are shown on the main profile page and mark exactly 1 publication from 2022 or later as 'Featured'. Success is measured by a setting that limits visible publications to 5 and one post-2021 publication flagged as featured.
steps: If not already signed in, use the site's 'Sign in' link and login form to log into the owner account, then open the user menu and choose 'Public profile settings' or 'Display settings'.
On the profile settings page, find the section labeled 'Publications display' or 'What to show on my profile' and locate the control for 'Number of publications to show'.
Set the 'Number of publications to show' field or slider to '5' so that only the first five items will appear on the main profile page.
Locate the 'Sorting' or 'Ordering' option for publications and choose 'Citations – High to Low' so that the most-cited publications are included in the top 5.

Scroll to a list or table of publications where individual items can be toggled as 'Featured' and apply a filter or sort to show only publications from the year 2022 and later (e.g., set the minimum year filter to '2022').
From the filtered list of 2022+ publications, select the first publication shown and click its 'Mark as featured', star icon, or 'Feature on profile' toggle to designate it as featured.
If any other publications are currently marked as featured, turn off their 'Featured' toggles so that exactly one publication remains featured.
Click the 'Save', 'Apply changes', or equivalent button within the profile settings page to store the limit of 5 most-cited publications and the single featured 2022+ publication.
